romanized: Shi la cha dan huang jiang zhan jiang

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sriracha mayo dip; closest parent is mayonnaise-based sauce (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers mayonnaise and emulsified sauces; sriracha mayo is a spiced variant within this category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 is the closest parent for mayonnaise-based preparations; no specific code for sriracha mayo dip.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of sriracha mayo dip is approximately 30 g, equivalent to about two tablespoons, served alongside pizza, fries, or other snack foods.

nutrient_source.title: Mayonnaise, regular (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 04025) and Sriracha hot sauce (USDA Branded Foods) — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sriracha mayo dip is not present as a discrete entry in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from a standard formulation of approximately 85% regular mayonnaise and 15% sriracha hot sauce by weight, using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for mayonnaise (NDB 04025, ~680 kcal/100g) and USDA Branded Foods data for sriracha sauce (~93 kcal/100g). Weighted composite yields the values reported below. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mayonnaise and fat-dominant condiments — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sriracha mayo dip. Mayonnaise and fat-dominant emulsified sauces have a very low GI (estimated 10–20) due to negligible carbohydrate content and high fat content, which slows gastric emptying. A GI of 15 is used as a conservative analogous estimate consistent with fat-dominant condiments reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (15 × 4.5 / 100) × 0.30 ≈ 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~580 mg/100g; ~174 mg per 30 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sriracha mayo dip to small portions (≤15 g) due to its significant sodium contribution from both mayonnaise and sriracha sauce, which can cumulatively exceed recommended daily sodium limits when combined with other salty foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g/100g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sriracha mayo dip has a negligible glycemic impact due to its very low carbohydrate content and fat-dominant composition; however, its high caloric density (~590 kcal/100g) means portion control remains important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing body weight.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (63 g/100g) including saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) and cholesterol (~38 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high fat content of sriracha mayo dip, particularly saturated fat from egg-yolk-based mayonnaise, warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; a serving of 15–20 g is preferable to limit saturated fat intake to under 2 g per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sriracha Mayo Dip is a creamy, spicy condiment made by blending mayonnaise with sriracha hot sauce, typically in a ratio of approximately 85:15 by weight. The result is a rich, emulsified dipping sauce with a smooth texture, a tangy heat from the sriracha chilli base, and the characteristic richness of egg-yolk mayonnaise. It is served cold or at room temperature as a dipping sauce alongside pizza, fries, chicken wings, spring rolls, and other finger foods. In Singapore, it is commonly offered as a side condiment at pizza and fast-casual restaurants, including outlets such as Now Pizza. Nutritionally, sriracha mayo dip is calorie-dense (~590 kcal per 100 g) and high in total fat (~63 g/100g), primarily from the polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fats in the mayonnaise base. Its carbohydrate content is very low (~4.5 g/100g), giving it a negligible glycemic impact (estimated GI ~15). Sodium content is notable (~580 mg/100g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The dip contains eggs as a primary allergen and may contain soy from the sriracha sauce. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans. A standard serving is approximately 30 g (two tablespoons), contributing roughly 177 kcal and 174 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sriracha mayo dip?
Is sriracha mayo high in sodium?
What allergens are in sriracha mayo dip?
Is sriracha mayo dip gluten free?

How much fat is in sriracha mayo dip?
Is sriracha mayo dip suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Sriracha mayo dip is not present as a discrete entry in any primary regional food composition database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for regular mayonnaise and USDA Branded Foods data for sriracha sauce, weighted by a standard formulation ratio. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across restaurants and the absence of a direct laboratory-analysed entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite formulation (approximately 85% regular mayonnaise, 15% sriracha hot sauce by weight) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 04025) and USDA Branded Foods data. Actual values may vary depending on the specific recipe used by Now Pizza - Funan or other restaurants. 2. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for a fat-dominant emulsified condiment; no direct GI measurement for sriracha mayo dip was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 3. Vitamin B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, and selenium are reported as -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported for this composite condiment in the available USDA source data. 4. Halal status is assumed based on standard commercial mayonnaise and sriracha ingredients; individual restaurant formulations should be verified. 5. The food_group is classified as Oils, seasonings and condiments consistent with its role as a dipping sauce rather than a main dish component. 6. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean cuisine context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Sriracha Mayo Dip'. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean cuisine rules. Local names are accurate and not promotional.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 590.0, protein_g 1.2, fat_g 63.0, carbohydrate_g 4.5) are present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 04025) composite estimate, clearly documented in nutrient_source. No forbidden sources used.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.2), sodium_mg (580.0), saturated_fat_g (9.5), and sugar_g (2.8) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a mayonnaise-based condiment.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is 15 (numeric, > 0), gi_category is 'low' (consistent with GI ≤ 55 threshold), glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.2, and gi_source references the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis is documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 (mayonnaise-based sauce, closest_parent), FoodEx2 A0CKP (mayonnaise and emulsified sauces, closest_parent), and SNOMED CT 229953004 (Mayonnaise substance, closest_parent) are all defensible and honest. All match_type values use only allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none). Gap notes explain why closest_parent is used.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context in Singapore, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, low GI and low carbs), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat). Each has human_condition_name, concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Type 2 Diabetes uses machine key type2_diabetes and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list contains 17 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated for all relevant languages. Every string in aliases_by_language has been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.

Iteration 2: Previous iteration produced invalid JSON (empty output from gemini). Rebuilt complete valid JSON.

Iteration 3: Verified all hard gates, confirmed aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list, confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes, confirmed GI category 'low' for GI=15 (≤55 threshold), confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings, confirmed zero-macro justification not needed as no core macro is zero.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — All six hard gates pass. Score 8/8. Sriracha mayo dip is a fat-dominant condiment served in Singapore. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (mayonnaise SR Legacy NDB 04025 + sriracha branded foods), clearly documented as estimated with medium confidence. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for fat-dominant emulsified condiments, sourced from University of Sydney GI Database convention and Atkinson et al. (2008). Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Ontology uses closest_parent for all three systems with honest gap notes. Health context covers Hypertension (high sodium concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low glycemic impact), and Cardiovascular Disease (saturated fat moderation). All sentinel -1 values documented in notes. Aliases consistency verified.
